headline: A San Francisco grocery platform buys its way into Western Canada
summary:
ABOUT THE COMPANY
SPUD is a Canadian online grocery service based in British Columbia that has delivered locally sourced food to households across B.C. and Alberta for more than two decades, positioning itself as an alternative to large supermarket chains by emphasizing local farms and ethical supply chains. GrubMarket, headquartered in San Francisco, operates an online farmers market and food distribution platform backed by venture capital, and has grown rapidly across the United States through a strategy of acquiring regional online food businesses rather than building them from scratch.

WHAT HAPPENED
GrubMarket has acquired SPUD, bringing one of Canada's longest-running online grocery services under American ownership. No purchase price was disclosed, and GrubMarket has not announced changes to SPUD's branding, operations, or staff. What the deal does confirm is that GrubMarket now has a direct foothold in British Columbia and Alberta, two provinces where online grocery adoption accelerated sharply during the pandemic and has not fully retreated since.

The timing is notable. Canadian grocery prices have remained elevated through 2025 and into 2026, and consumers in B.C. and Alberta have increasingly turned to alternatives to the dominant national chains, Loblaw, Sobeys, and Metro, which together control the vast majority of Canadian grocery retail. SPUD built its reputation by connecting those consumers to local producers, a model that attracted a loyal customer base but that has always struggled to achieve the scale needed to compete on price. GrubMarket's acquisition suggests it sees value in that loyalty and in SPUD's supplier relationships, even if the financial terms suggest this was not a blockbuster exit for SPUD's backers.

The broader backdrop matters here. Canada is navigating a difficult moment in its food economy. The United States has announced 50 percent tariffs on Canadian dairy and other agricultural products, effective August 2026, and retaliatory pressures are reshaping where Canadian food producers look for customers and partners. American investment flowing into Canadian food distribution, even through a relatively quiet acquisition like this one, signals that U.S. capital still sees Canadian consumers and Canadian supply chains as attractive, tariff disputes or not.

WHY IT MATTERS
This deal changes SPUD's competitive position in a specific way: it now has access to GrubMarket's logistics technology, supplier network, and capital, which could help it lower costs or expand its product range. For the local farmers and food producers in B.C. and Alberta who rely on SPUD as a distribution channel, American ownership introduces a new question about whether a San Francisco-based parent will prioritize local sourcing or gradually shift toward GrubMarket's existing U.S. supplier base. For the Canadian online grocery sector broadly, this acquisition reinforces a pattern: homegrown platforms that achieve real traction tend to get acquired by better-capitalized American players rather than scaling independently.

WHAT OTHERS CAN LEARN
Canadian food entrepreneurs who have built platforms connecting local producers to consumers should be watching this deal carefully, not because GrubMarket is a threat, but because it confirms that American acquirers are actively scanning Canada for established customer relationships and regional supply networks. If you run a local food distribution business and want to grow on your own terms rather than be acquired, Export Development Canada's agri-food trade team can help you find export customers that reduce your dependence on domestic margins. The Grain Farmers of Ontario's Innovation Fund, which is currently accepting applications for projects up to $75,000, is one example of sector funding that can help food businesses invest in the technology and logistics improvements that make them harder to displace, and more valuable if they choose to sell.

headline: A former hockey player raises $1.7 million to solve what sport never does: what comes next
summary:
ABOUT THE COMPANY
Kirsten Toth is a former professional hockey player turned founder who has raised seed funding to build a social networking platform designed specifically for athletes navigating the transition out of competitive sport. The company does not yet have a widely publicized brand name, but the platform is positioned as a career and community tool for athletes who have spent their formative years training rather than building the professional networks that most knowledge-economy workers accumulate through university programs or early-career jobs.

WHAT HAPPENED
Toth has closed a $1.7 million U.S. seed round to develop and launch her platform. The funding will support product development and, presumably, early community building among athlete networks. The round is small by the standards of major technology raises, but it is meaningful for a founder at this stage, and it signals that investors see a real market in a problem that has received almost no dedicated technology attention: the career cliff that most professional and semi-professional athletes face when their playing days end.

The problem Toth is trying to solve is well documented even if it rarely surfaces in business coverage. Athletes who compete at high levels, whether in hockey, basketball, soccer, or other sports, typically spend the years between 16 and 30 in a world structured entirely around performance. They rarely build LinkedIn profiles, attend industry conferences, or accumulate the informal professional contacts that most Canadians rely on when changing careers. When sport ends, often abruptly due to injury or contract decisions, they face the labour market without a conventional network. Toth experienced this firsthand and is building the infrastructure she wished had existed.

Canada is a logical home base for this kind of platform. The country produces a disproportionate share of the world's professional hockey players, and the hockey pipeline, from minor hockey to major junior to professional leagues, runs through hundreds of smaller Canadian cities and towns. Beyond hockey, Canada fields strong national programs in soccer, basketball, athletics, and winter sports, producing thousands of athletes annually who will eventually need to transition into second careers. The platform's potential user base extends well beyond Canada, but the density of hockey culture here gives Toth a credible and emotionally resonant starting community.

WHY IT MATTERS
The $1.7 million raise does two things worth watching. First, it puts capital behind a founder who is solving a problem from direct lived experience, which tends to produce more durable products than those built by outsiders guessing at user needs. Second, it adds a new kind of platform to Canada's technology sector: one focused on identity and career transition for a population that is large, globally distributed, and deeply networked within sport but poorly connected outside it. If the platform works, it could become a meaningful professional tool for tens of thousands of current and former athletes in Canada and internationally, and it could generate valuable data about second-career pathways that has never been systematically collected.

WHAT OTHERS CAN LEARN
Founders building niche professional networks or community platforms should note that Toth's raise came from a clearly defined user problem with a credible founder at the centre. Investors at the seed stage, particularly in Canada, are increasingly funding founders who bring domain expertise and lived experience rather than purely technical backgrounds. If you are an athlete, coach, or sports administrator with an idea for a platform or service that addresses a gap you have personally experienced, the National Research Council of Canada's Industrial Research Assistance Program, known as IRAP, provides funding and advisory support to early-stage Canadian companies developing technology products and is worth a conversation before you approach venture capital.
